title: ResNet & Deep CNN Architectures
summary: Residual connections originated in CNNs (He et al., 2015). Enabled training 100+ layer networks. Same principle as transformer residuals: $X_{\text{out}} = X + F(X)$. Also covers BatchNorm vs LayerNorm, bottleneck blocks, and depthwise separable convolutions.
key_insights: Before ResNet: deeper = worse (even on training set). Degradation problem, not overfitting.
Key insight: if identity mapping is optimal, learning $F(X) = 0$ is easier than learning $F(X) = X$
ResNet-50 has 4 stages with bottleneck blocks: $1 \times 1$ compress → $3 \times 3$ conv → $1 \times 1$ expand. Reduces FLOPs.
BatchNorm in CNNs normalizes across batch + spatial dims. LayerNorm in transformers normalizes across feature dim. Different axes, same principle.
Depthwise separable conv: split spatial and channel mixing. $k^2 + C_{\text{out}}$ params instead of $k^2 C_{\text{out}}$. Used in MobileNet.
Skip connections in ResNet are the direct ancestor of transformer residual connections.

mathematical_core.residual_block: $$X_{\text{out}} = X + F(X, \{W_i\})$$ where $F$ = conv → BN → ReLU → conv → BN
mathematical_core.depthwise_separable: Depthwise: $k \times k$ conv per channel ($C \cdot k^2$ params). Pointwise: $1 \times 1$ conv across channels ($C_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{out}}$ params). Total: $C(k^2 + C_{\text{out}})$ vs standard $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$.

derivations.title: BatchNorm vs LayerNorm — what axis and why it matters

steps.name: BatchNorm
steps.math: Normalize across batch dimension for each feature/channel. $\mu, \sigma$ computed per channel over all batch items and spatial positions. Requires batch statistics → batch-size dependent.

steps.name: LayerNorm
steps.math: Normalize across feature dimension for each sample. $\mu, \sigma$ computed per token/sample over all features. Batch-size independent.

steps.name: Why CNNs use BN, transformers use LN
steps.math: CNNs: same feature should have consistent statistics across images → normalize across batch. Transformers: sequence lengths vary, batch dimension is less meaningful → normalize across features.

derivations.consequence: BN needs large batches (≥32) to estimate stable statistics. LN works with batch size 1. This is why transformers use LN.

geometric_properties: Residual learning: $F(X)$ learns the delta, not the full mapping. Easier to learn near-identity.
Bottleneck: compress to lower dim → conv in lower dim → expand back. Cheaper than full-dim conv.
statistical_properties: BN running statistics drift during training — momentum parameter controls update speed
BN at eval uses running mean/var (not batch stats). Training-eval mismatch is a known issue.
numerical_properties: ResNet-50: 25.6M params, 4.1 GFLOPs. ResNet-152: 60.2M params, 11.6 GFLOPs.
Bottleneck saves ~3.7× FLOPs vs standard block at same depth.
MobileNet (depthwise separable): 3.4M params, 569 MFLOPs. ~8-9× cheaper than ResNet for similar accuracy.

enables: CNN.VIT — ViT compared against ResNet baselines
